title: Head and Neck Pathology Sign-Out Simulations

cases.title: Oral tongue biopsy: invasive squamous cell carcinoma
cases.clinicalHistory: 61-year-old man with tobacco history and a nonhealing ulcer on the lateral oral tongue.

image.caption: Well-differentiated oral squamous cell carcinoma with keratinizing invasion.

sourceArticle.figureCaption: histological slides a scc h e 100 at surface of tumour demonstrating irregular nests of highly atypical squamous cells invading the submucosal space with adjacent oral mucosa at top left b scc h e 100 present as infiltrative tumoural nests with keratinisation at invasive front of tumour c lymph node metastasis h e 20 demonstrating extranodal extension of scc in to adjacent soft tissue

diagnosticSteps: Confirm destructive stromal invasion by atypical squamous nests or cords rather than tangentially sectioned dysplasia.
Assess tumor differentiation, keratinization, depth represented in the biopsy, and perineural or lymphovascular invasion if present.
Evaluate adjacent mucosa for high-grade dysplasia and state biopsy limitations for depth of invasion.

ancillaryOptions.name: p40 or p63
ancillaryOptions.result: Confirms squamous lineage if the tumor is poorly differentiated.

ancillaryOptions.name: p16
ancillaryOptions.result: Not a routine surrogate for HPV-driven disease in oral cavity SCC.

ancillaryOptions.name: GMS stain
ancillaryOptions.result: Consider only if pseudoepitheliomatous hyperplasia with fungal infection is a realistic mimic.

cases.reportingTarget: Tongue, lateral, biopsy: Invasive keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma, moderately differentiated. Depth of invasion cannot be reliably determined in this biopsy.
pitfalls: Do not assign final depth of invasion from a superficial or fragmented incisional biopsy.
Do not use p16 positivity alone to reclassify an oral cavity primary as HPV-associated oropharyngeal carcinoma.

cases.title: Tonsillectomy: HPV-associated oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma
cases.clinicalHistory: 55-year-old man with cystic level II neck node and asymmetric right tonsil; biopsy showed nonkeratinizing squamous cell carcinoma.

image.caption: Nonkeratinizing squamous cell carcinoma compatible with HPV-associated oropharyngeal primary in the proper context.

sourceArticle.figureCaption: 73 year old man with an human papilloma virus hpv 16 positive squamous cell carcinoma from an unknown primary site with bilateral positive level 2 lymph nodes the patient was treated with deintensified intensity modulated radiotherapy imrt to 60 gy in 30 once daily fractions to the oropharynx and bilateral positive nodes and 54 gy in 30 fractions to the retropharyngeal nodes on the side with bulkier neck disease and levels 3 and 4 bilaterally concomitant weekly cisplatin 30 mg m2 was administered the patient tolerated treatment well and has remained disease free for 4 5 years without complications

diagnosticSteps: Confirm nonkeratinizing or basaloid squamous carcinoma arising in oropharyngeal tonsillar tissue.
Verify HPV association with block-type p16 staining or HPV-specific testing according to local practice.
Report tumor size, margin status, lymphovascular invasion, perineural invasion, nodal metastases, and extranodal extension.

ancillaryOptions.name: p16 immunohistochemistry
ancillaryOptions.result: Strong diffuse block-type p16 supports HPV-associated oropharyngeal SCC.

ancillaryOptions.name: High-risk HPV RNA/DNA in situ hybridization
ancillaryOptions.result: Confirms transcriptionally active or high-risk HPV when p16 is equivocal or context is unusual.

ancillaryOptions.name: EBER in situ hybridization
ancillaryOptions.result: Use if nasopharyngeal carcinoma is a serious differential diagnosis.

cases.reportingTarget: Right tonsil, radical tonsillectomy: HPV-associated squamous cell carcinoma, 2.3 cm, margins negative. Neck dissection: metastatic carcinoma in 2 of 18 lymph nodes, largest deposit 3.1 cm, no extranodal extension, pT2 pN1.
pitfalls: Do not apply HPV-associated staging to a non-oropharyngeal primary.
Do not omit extranodal extension assessment in nodal disease.

cases.title: Total laryngectomy: transglottic squamous cell carcinoma
cases.clinicalHistory: 68-year-old man with progressive hoarseness, airway compromise, and imaging showing a transglottic mass with thyroid cartilage erosion.

image.caption: Keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma of the larynx.

sourceArticle.figureCaption: a forest plot of multivariable disease free survival analysis dfs survival curves derived from the multivariable dfs model showing the marginal effect of the histological type b stage c and treatment d adjusted for the effects of other covariates legend scc squamous cell carcinoma tlm transoral laser microsurgery ophl open partial horizontal laryngectomy ctra crico tracheal resection and anastomosis tl total laryngectomy

diagnosticSteps: Map the tumor across supraglottic, glottic, and subglottic subsites and correlate with gross cartilage involvement.
Confirm invasion pattern, histologic grade, lymphovascular invasion, perineural invasion, and thyroid cartilage or extralaryngeal extension.
Assess mucosal, soft tissue, tracheal, and pharyngeal margins and stage using the involved laryngeal subsite.

ancillaryOptions.name: No ancillary study
ancillaryOptions.result: Typical keratinizing laryngeal SCC is signed out on morphology and anatomic extent.

ancillaryOptions.name: p40
ancillaryOptions.result: Useful if the carcinoma is poorly differentiated and lineage is uncertain.

ancillaryOptions.name: p16
ancillaryOptions.result: Not routinely used for staging laryngeal SCC.

cases.reportingTarget: Larynx, total laryngectomy: Keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma, moderately differentiated, 3.8 cm, transglottic, invading through thyroid cartilage, margins negative, pT4a.
pitfalls: Do not stage cartilage invasion from decalcified sections without careful gross-radiologic correlation.
Do not omit specific margin sites in a laryngectomy specimen.

cases.title: Thyroidectomy: classic papillary thyroid carcinoma
cases.specimenType: Total thyroidectomy with central neck lymph nodes
cases.clinicalHistory: 42-year-old woman with a 2.1 cm hypoechoic thyroid nodule with microcalcifications and Bethesda VI FNA.

image.caption: Papillary thyroid carcinoma with papillary architecture and diagnostic nuclear features.

sourceArticle.figureCaption: evidence of lymph node metastasis in a case of completion thyroidectomy 200x h e

diagnosticSteps: Confirm papillary thyroid carcinoma by nuclear enlargement, clearing, grooves, pseudoinclusions, and papillary or infiltrative architecture.
Measure tumor size and determine focality, extrathyroidal extension, vascular invasion, lymphatic invasion, and margin status.
Assess submitted lymph nodes and report number positive, size of largest metastatic deposit, and extranodal extension.

ancillaryOptions.name: No ancillary study
ancillaryOptions.result: Classic papillary thyroid carcinoma is usually diagnosed morphologically.

ancillaryOptions.name: BRAF V600E testing
ancillaryOptions.result: May be ordered for risk stratification or targeted therapy context but is not required for routine diagnosis.

ancillaryOptions.name: Thyroglobulin and TTF-1
ancillaryOptions.result: Useful if metastatic thyroid origin is uncertain.

cases.reportingTarget: Thyroid, total thyroidectomy: Papillary thyroid carcinoma, classic subtype, 2.1 cm, confined to thyroid, margins negative. Central neck: metastatic carcinoma in 1 of 6 lymph nodes, 0.2 cm deposit, no extranodal extension, pT2 pN1a.
pitfalls: Do not diagnose papillary carcinoma from architecture alone without diagnostic nuclear features.
Do not omit microscopic extrathyroidal extension and margin relationship.

cases.title: Superficial parotidectomy: pleomorphic adenoma
cases.clinicalHistory: 49-year-old woman with a slow-growing, mobile 2.7 cm parotid tail mass and benign salivary neoplasm on FNA.

image.caption: Pleomorphic adenoma with epithelial and myxochondroid stromal components.

sourceArticle.figureCaption: presurgical imaging coronal image t1 weighted fat saturated of a 54 year old patient with a smaller contrast enhancing lesion in the middle part of the parotid gland histologically proven as a pa marked by an arrow and a second less enhancing lesion in the parotid gland histologically proven as a wt marked by an asterisk pa pleomorphic adenoma wt warthin s tumor

diagnosticSteps: Recognize biphasic ductal and myoepithelial proliferation in myxoid or chondromyxoid stroma.
Evaluate capsule, pseudopodia, tumor spillage, and margin distance because recurrence risk depends on excision quality.
Search for carcinoma ex pleomorphic adenoma features including infiltrative growth, marked atypia, necrosis, and high mitotic activity.

ancillaryOptions.name: No ancillary study
ancillaryOptions.result: Typical pleomorphic adenoma is diagnosed morphologically.

ancillaryOptions.name: PLAG1 or HMGA2 testing
ancillaryOptions.result: Can support diagnosis in unusual cellular or limited samples.

ancillaryOptions.name: p63, SOX10, S100, calponin
ancillaryOptions.result: Can highlight myoepithelial differentiation if morphology is ambiguous.

synopticChecklist: carcinoma ex pleomorphic adenoma absent or present
cases.reportingTarget: Parotid gland, superficial parotidectomy: Pleomorphic adenoma, 2.7 cm, narrowly excised with tumor 0.1 cm from inked margin; no carcinoma identified.
pitfalls: Do not ignore a small frankly invasive carcinoma component in a long-standing pleomorphic adenoma.
Do not overcall pseudopod-like capsular extension as malignant invasion.

cases.title: Tonsils and adenoids: lymphoid hyperplasia, gross-only workflow
cases.clinicalHistory: 7-year-old child with obstructive sleep symptoms and symmetric tonsillar hypertrophy; no mass, ulceration, or malignancy concern.

image.caption: Symmetric tonsillar tissue without a described suspicious mass lesion.

diagnosticSteps: Confirm pediatric obstructive indication with symmetric tonsils/adenoids and no clinical red flags.
Inspect for asymmetry, ulceration, firm mass, necrosis, or abnormal color that would trigger histologic submission.
Document laterality, dimensions, aggregate adenoid tissue, and absence of gross lesion according to local gross-only policy.

ancillaryOptions.name: No ancillary study
ancillaryOptions.result: No ancillary study is required for uncomplicated symmetric pediatric tonsils/adenoids.

ancillaryOptions.name: Flow cytometry
ancillaryOptions.result: Use only when lymphoma is clinically or grossly suspected, or for marked asymmetry with concerning features.

ancillaryOptions.name: Representative histology
ancillaryOptions.result: Submit tissue if gross findings or clinical history fall outside gross-only criteria.

cases.reportingTarget: Tonsils and adenoids, excision: Symmetric tonsillar and adenoidal lymphoid tissue by gross examination; no gross lesion identified.
pitfalls: Do not gross-only an adult tonsillectomy or asymmetric tonsil unless local policy explicitly permits it.
Do not ignore a firm, ulcerated, or necrotic focus.
